regulations.title: Practical Approaches to Risk Minimisation for Medicinal Products
regulations.summaryDescription: CIOMS guidance on practical implementation of risk minimisation measures. Provides framework for selecting, implementing, and evaluating effectiveness of routine and additional RMMs. Complements GVP Module XVI and FDA REMS guidance with practical recommendations.
keyRequirements:
1. RMM SELECTION FRAMEWORK:
   - Risk characterization
   - Behaviour change objectives
   - Target audience identification
   - Tool selection criteria
   - Proportionality assessment

2. ROUTINE RMMs:
   - SmPC/PI optimization
   - Package leaflet design
   - Pack size restrictions
   - Legal supply status

3. ADDITIONAL RMMs:
   - Educational materials
   - HCP training
   - Patient registries
   - Controlled distribution
   - Pregnancy prevention programmes

4. EFFECTIVENESS EVALUATION:
   - Process indicators
   - Outcome indicators
   - Survey methodologies
   - Claims data analysis

5. GLOBAL IMPLEMENTATION:
   - Regional adaptation
   - Cultural considerations
   - Resource constraints
   - Stakeholder engagement
regulations.notes: International - Risk minimisation measure design and evaluation

regulations.title: Patient Involvement in Drug Development and Safe Use of Medicines
regulations.summaryDescription: CIOMS guidance on involving patients throughout drug development and pharmacovigilance. Addresses patient reporting, participation in benefit-risk assessment, and engagement in safety communications. Reflects growing regulatory emphasis on patient perspective in PV.
keyRequirements:
1. PATIENT REPORTING:
   - Direct patient ADR reporting
   - Patient perspectives in ICSRs
   - Quality of patient reports
   - Integration with HCP reports

2. BENEFIT-RISK ASSESSMENT:
   - Patient preference studies
   - Patient-relevant outcomes
   - Risk tolerance research
   - Patient input in regulatory decisions

3. SAFETY COMMUNICATION:
   - Patient-friendly language
   - Health literacy considerations
   - Digital communication channels
   - Co-creation with patients

4. CLINICAL DEVELOPMENT:
   - Patient input in protocol design
   - PRO (patient-reported outcomes)
   - Trial burden considerations
   - Informed consent improvements

5. RMM DESIGN:
   - Patient perspective in RMM development
   - Usability testing
   - Effectiveness measurement from patient view
regulations.notes: International - Patient engagement in pharmacovigilance

regulations.title: Identification of Medicinal Products Standards
regulations.summaryDescription: Suite of five ISO standards for unique identification of medicinal products globally. Enables accurate product identification in ICSRs, supply chain, and regulatory submissions. EU is most advanced in implementation via SPOR. Foundation for global PV data interoperability.
keyRequirements:
1. ISO IDMP STANDARDS:
   - ISO 11615: Medicinal product identification (PhPID)
   - ISO 11616: Pharmaceutical product identification
   - ISO 11238: Substance identification (SubID)
   - ISO 11239: Pharmaceutical dose forms, routes, units
   - ISO 11240: Units of measurement

2. SUBSTANCE IDENTIFICATION (11238):
   - Unique substance identifiers (SubID)
   - Active ingredients
   - Excipients
   - Basis for ICSR drug identification

3. PRODUCT IDENTIFICATION (11615/11616):
   - Medicinal Product Identifier (MPID)
   - Pharmaceutical Product Identifier (PhPID)
   - Hierarchical identification

4. EU IMPLEMENTATION:
   - SPOR programme implements IDMP
   - XEVMPD alignment
   - Article 57 data migration
   - Phased implementation timeline

5. PV RELEVANCE:
   - Accurate ICSR product identification
   - Cross-border safety data matching
   - Signal detection accuracy
   - Global data interoperability
regulations.notes: International - Global product identification standard; EU SPOR implementation

regulations.title: Changes to an Approved Biologics License Application (BLA)
regulations.summaryDescription: Establishes requirements for changes to approved BLAs, including safety-related labeling changes. Parallel regulation to 21 CFR 314.70 for biologics. Provides CBE (Changes Being Effected) mechanism for immediate implementation of safety labeling updates. Critical for biologics PV - enables rapid labeling response to emerging safety signals.
keyRequirements:
1. SUPPLEMENT CATEGORIES:
   - Prior Approval Supplement: Major changes requiring FDA approval before implementation
   - CBE-30: Changes effective 30 days after submission unless FDA objects
   - CBE-0: Changes effective immediately upon submission (safety labeling)
   - Annual Report: Minor changes

2. SAFETY LABELING CHANGES - 601.12(f)(2):
   - Add/strengthen contraindication, warning, precaution, or adverse reaction: CBE-0
   - Add/strengthen dosage and administration info for safety: CBE-0
   - Add/strengthen statement about drug abuse, dependence, overdosage: CBE-0
   - Delete false/misleading safety claims: CBE-0

3. MANUFACTURING CHANGES:
   - May trigger safety assessment
   - Comparability protocols for biologics
   - Process changes affecting product quality

4. LABELING CONTENT:
   - Must comply with 21 CFR 201.56/57 (PLR format)
   - Prescribing information requirements
   - Patient labeling requirements

5. INSPECTION RELEVANCE:
   - FDA may cite failure to update labeling promptly
   - Delay in safety labeling updates is compliance issue
   - Must have procedures for labeling change assessment
regulations.notes: United States - All licensed biological products; Parallel to 21 CFR 314.70 for drugs

regulations.title: General Requirements for Records and Reports - CGMP
regulations.summaryDescription: Establishes general requirements for CGMP records including retention periods. Critical for PV because complaint files and safety-related records must be retained per this section. Frequently cited in FDA inspections when records are incomplete or unavailable. Includes requirement for annual product review which may identify safety trends.
keyRequirements:
1. RECORD RETENTION - 211.180(a):
   - Records required by this part: 1 year after batch expiration
   - OTC drugs without expiration: 3 years after distribution
   - Records of complaints: Per 211.198 (1 year after expiry or 3 years)

2. ANNUAL PRODUCT REVIEW - 211.180(e):
   - Written procedures for annual evaluation
   - Review of complaints (PV-relevant)
   - Review of deviations and investigations
   - Review of changes to product/process
   - Review of variations in product quality
   - Determine need for revalidation

3. RECORD AVAILABILITY:
   - Records readily available for inspection
   - Legible and stored to prevent deterioration
   - Electronic records per 21 CFR Part 11

4. PV RELEVANCE:
   - Complaint records retention critical
   - Annual review may identify safety signals
   - Investigation records must be complete
   - Audit trail for safety-related decisions

5. INSPECTION FINDINGS:
   - Missing/incomplete complaint files cited
   - Failure to conduct annual review
   - Records not readily available
regulations.notes: United States - All drug manufacturers; Record retention requirements including complaints

regulations.title: General Requirements for Laboratory Controls - CGMP
regulations.summaryDescription: Establishes laboratory control requirements including scientifically sound specifications and test procedures. PV relevance: Out-of-specification results may indicate product quality issues that could cause adverse events. OOS investigations per 211.192 may reveal safety-impacting problems. Laboratory data integrity is critical for product safety assurance.
keyRequirements:
1. SPECIFICATIONS AND STANDARDS - 211.160(a):
   - Scientifically sound and appropriate specifications
   - Standards, sampling plans, and test procedures
   - Designed to assure drug products meet requirements

2. LABORATORY CONTROLS - 211.160(b):
   - Include determination of conformance to specifications
   - Include appropriate laboratory testing
   - Scientific rationale for specifications
   - Sampling must be representative

3. PV RELEVANCE:
   - OOS results may indicate safety issues
   - Potency failures: Sub/super-potent = AE risk
   - Impurity exceedances: Toxicity concerns
   - Stability failures: Degradation products safety
   - Sterility failures: Contamination events

4. OOS INVESTIGATION:
   - Must investigate per 211.192
   - Cannot reject without investigation
   - Safety assessment if distributed
   - Field alert if safety impact

5. DATA INTEGRITY:
   - Laboratory data subject to Part 11
   - Audit trail requirements
   - No deletion of failing results
regulations.notes: United States - Laboratory testing requirements; Indirect PV relevance through product quality

regulations.title: FDA Sentinel System - Active Post-Market Risk Identification and Analysis
regulations.summaryDescription: FDA's national electronic system for monitoring safety of FDA-regulated medical products using healthcare data from multiple sources. Established under FDAAA 2007. Uses distributed data approach accessing 100+ million patient records. MAHs should understand Sentinel as FDA may use Sentinel data to support safety actions, require PMRs, or challenge company signal assessments.
keyRequirements:
1. SENTINEL CAPABILITIES:
   - Active surveillance (vs passive FAERS)
   - Access to claims/EHR data from data partners
   - 100+ million patients covered
   - Near real-time signal detection
   - Sequential analysis methods

2. DATA SOURCES:
   - Commercial health plans
   - Medicare/Medicaid
   - Integrated delivery systems
   - EHR data

3. USE CASES:
   - Signal refinement from FAERS
   - Pre-specified safety studies
   - PMR/PMC support
   - Label change support
   - REMS evaluation

4. MAH RELEVANCE:
   - FDA may cite Sentinel findings in safety actions
   - May be required to conduct Sentinel-based PMR
   - Should understand methodology for responding to FDA queries
   - Sentinel data may contradict/confirm company findings

5. INSPECTION IMPLICATIONS:
   - FDA may ask about company's own active surveillance
   - May compare to Sentinel findings
   - Signal detection capabilities scrutinized
regulations.notes: United States - FDA active surveillance system; MAHs should understand FDA's signal detection capabilities

regulations.officialIdentifier: FDA Guidance: Establishing Pregnancy Exposure Registries
regulations.title: Establishing Pregnancy Exposure Registries - FDA Guidance
regulations.summaryDescription: FDA guidance on designing and conducting pregnancy exposure registries to collect data on outcomes when pregnant women are exposed to drugs. Often required as PMC or PMR condition. Critical for products with teratogenic potential or insufficient pregnancy data. Registry data supports labeling updates under PLLR (Pregnancy and Lactation Labeling Rule).
keyRequirements:
1. REGISTRY DESIGN:
   - Prospective enrollment (before outcome known)
   - Comparison group (unexposed or disease-matched)
   - Defined outcomes (major malformations, spontaneous abortion, etc.)
   - Adequate sample size for signal detection

2. ENROLLMENT:
   - Healthcare provider or patient enrollment
   - First trimester exposure prioritized
   - Collect timing, dose, duration, indication
   - Follow to pregnancy outcome

3. OUTCOMES ASSESSED:
   - Major congenital malformations
   - Spontaneous abortion
   - Stillbirth
   - Preterm birth
   - Small for gestational age
   - Neonatal outcomes

4. DATA ANALYSIS:
   - Compare to background rates
   - Adjust for confounders
   - Signal detection for specific malformations
   - Cumulative analysis

5. LABELING IMPLICATIONS:
   - Informs PLLR pregnancy subsection
   - Human data vs animal data
   - Risk communication to prescribers
   - Registry contact information in labeling

6. REPORTING:
   - Annual interim reports to FDA
   - Final report at registry completion
   - Publication of findings
regulations.notes: United States - Pregnancy registry requirements; Often required as PMC/PMR

regulations.title: Drug Safety Communications - FDA Public Safety Announcements
regulations.summaryDescription: FDA's primary mechanism for communicating emerging safety information to healthcare professionals and patients. DSCs may announce new safety information, ongoing safety reviews, labeling changes, or completed safety reviews. MAHs must monitor DSCs affecting their products and may be required to take action (labeling updates, DHPC, etc.).
keyRequirements:
1. DSC TYPES:
   - Initial DSC: New safety concern identified
   - Update DSC: Ongoing review with new information
   - Final DSC: Completed review with conclusions
   - Labeling change DSC: Announcing label update

2. DSC CONTENT:
   - Safety issue description
   - Recommendations for HCPs/patients
   - What FDA is doing
   - What patients/HCPs should do
   - Reporting instructions (MedWatch)

3. MAH RESPONSIBILITIES:
   - Monitor FDA DSCs for products
   - Assess implications for labeling
   - Prepare DHPC if warranted
   - Update labeling per FDA direction
   - Respond to FDA queries

4. DISTRIBUTION:
   - FDA website
   - Email alerts (subscribers)
   - MedWatch
   - Social media
   - Press release for significant issues

5. INSPECTION RELEVANCE:
   - FDA may ask about response to DSC
   - Failure to update labeling after DSC is finding
   - Should have process to monitor DSCs
regulations.notes: United States - FDA public safety communications; MAH must monitor and respond

regulations.title: MedWatch: FDA Safety Reporting Program - Forms and Instructions
regulations.summaryDescription: FDA's safety reporting forms and submission guidance. Form 3500 (voluntary) for HCPs/consumers; Form 3500A (mandatory) for industry. Understanding proper form completion is critical for compliance. Inspection findings may cite incomplete or incorrect form submissions.
keyRequirements:
1. FORM 3500 (Voluntary):
   - Healthcare professionals
   - Consumers/patients
   - Online, mail, fax, phone
   - Feeds to FAERS

2. FORM 3500A (Mandatory):
   - Manufacturers, packers, distributors
   - Required by regulation
   - Electronic (E2B) or paper
   - Specific data elements required

3. KEY DATA ELEMENTS:
   - Patient information (age, sex, weight)
   - Adverse event description
   - Suspect product details
   - Concomitant medications
   - Relevant history
   - Reporter information
   - Outcome

4. COMMON ERRORS (Inspection Findings):
   - Incomplete patient demographics
   - Missing event onset dates
   - Inadequate event descriptions
   - Missing reporter contact
   - Incorrect seriousness coding
   - Late submissions

5. ELECTRONIC SUBMISSION:
   - E2B(R3) format required
   - FDA ESG gateway
   - Acknowledgments monitored
   - Rejections corrected promptly
regulations.notes: United States - MedWatch form completion and submission requirements

regulations.officialIdentifier: FDA Guidance: Safety Considerations for Biosimilars
regulations.title: Clinical Pharmacology Data to Support Biosimilar Products - Safety Considerations
regulations.summaryDescription: FDA guidance on safety considerations for biosimilar development and post-approval monitoring. Emphasizes immunogenicity assessment as key safety differentiator from reference product. PV implications include enhanced immunogenicity monitoring, potential for product-specific adverse events, and interchangeability safety considerations.
keyRequirements:
1. IMMUNOGENICITY ASSESSMENT:
   - Pre-approval immunogenicity studies
   - Comparison to reference product
   - Anti-drug antibody (ADA) testing
   - Neutralizing antibody assessment
   - Clinical impact of immunogenicity

2. POST-APPROVAL PV:
   - Enhanced immunogenicity monitoring
   - Product-specific AE tracking
   - Signal detection for immunogenic events
   - Traceability requirements

3. INTERCHANGEABILITY:
   - Additional switching studies if seeking designation
   - Safety monitoring in switches
   - Pharmacovigilance for interchangeable products

4. TRACEABILITY:
   - Must be able to trace AEs to specific biosimilar
   - Product naming conventions
   - Batch-level tracking

5. LABELING:
   - Biosimilar-specific safety information
   - Reference product comparison
   - Immunogenicity data presentation

6. PMR/PMC:
   - May require post-approval immunogenicity study
   - Registry for immunogenic events
   - Long-term safety follow-up
regulations.notes: United States - Biosimilar-specific safety requirements; Immunogenicity focus

regulations.title: How to Comply with the Pediatric Research Equity Act (PREA) - Safety Requirements
regulations.summaryDescription: FDA guidance on pediatric safety requirements under PREA (Pediatric Research Equity Act). Requires pediatric studies for drugs likely to be used in children. Specific safety considerations for developmental effects, age-appropriate formulations, and long-term follow-up. PV must include pediatric-specific signal detection and reporting.
keyRequirements:
1. PREA REQUIREMENTS:
   - Pediatric assessments required unless waived/deferred
   - Applies to NDAs, BLAs, efficacy supplements
   - Must study all relevant pediatric age groups
   - Safety assessment for each age group

2. PEDIATRIC AGE GROUPS:
   - Neonates (birth to <1 month)
   - Infants (1 month to <2 years)
   - Children (2 to <12 years)
   - Adolescents (12 to <17 years)

3. SAFETY CONSIDERATIONS:
   - Developmental toxicity
   - Growth and maturation effects
   - Age-specific adverse reactions
   - Long-term safety follow-up
   - Off-label use monitoring

4. PV REQUIREMENTS:
   - Pediatric-specific signal detection
   - Age-stratified analysis in PSURs
   - Developmental outcome monitoring
   - Growth parameter tracking

5. LABELING:
   - Pediatric Use section (8.4)
   - Age-specific dosing
   - Pediatric-specific warnings
   - Formulation information

6. PMR/PMC:
   - May require pediatric PMR
   - Long-term safety studies
   - Registries for pediatric use
regulations.notes: United States - Pediatric-specific safety requirements under PREA

regulations.title: Electronic Records; Electronic Signatures
regulations.summaryDescription: FDA regulation governing electronic records and electronic signatures. Directly applicable to PV databases, safety reporting systems, and electronic case processing. Requires validation of computerized systems, audit trails, access controls, and electronic signature standards. Frequently cited in PV system inspections.
keyRequirements:
1. ELECTRONIC RECORDS - 11.10:
   - Validation of systems for accuracy, reliability
   - Ability to generate accurate and complete copies
   - Protection of records throughout retention period
   - Limiting system access to authorized individuals
   - Secure, computer-generated audit trails
   - Operational system checks
   - Authority checks (user permissions)
   - Device checks where appropriate
   - Training documentation
   - Written policies for electronic signatures

2. AUDIT TRAILS - 11.10(e):
   - Secure, computer-generated, time-stamped
   - Record date/time of operator entries
   - Record identity of operator
   - Cannot be modified by ordinary means
   - Available for agency review and copying

3. ELECTRONIC SIGNATURES - 11.50/11.70:
   - Unique to one individual
   - Not reused or reassigned
   - Two distinct identification components
   - Signature manifestation requirements

4. PV SYSTEM APPLICABILITY:
   - Safety databases
   - Case processing systems
   - Signal detection tools
   - Document management systems
   - ICSR submission systems

5. INSPECTION FOCUS:
   - System validation documentation
   - Audit trail configuration and review
   - Access control and user management
   - Data integrity controls
regulations.notes: United States - Electronic records and signatures for PV databases and systems

regulations.title: REMS: FDA's Application of Statutory Factors in Determining When a REMS is Necessary
regulations.summaryDescription: FDA guidance on statutory factors for determining when REMS is necessary and requirements for REMS programs. Includes shared system REMS requirements (e.g., opioid REMS). MAHs must understand REMS trigger criteria and operational requirements. Inspection may assess REMS implementation and effectiveness.
keyRequirements:
1. STATUTORY FACTORS - FDCA 505-1(a):
   - Size of population likely to use
   - Seriousness of disease/condition
   - Expected benefit of drug
   - Expected/actual duration of treatment
   - Seriousness of known/potential adverse events
   - Whether drug is new molecular entity

2. REMS ELEMENTS:
   - Medication Guide and/or PPI
   - Communication Plan
   - Elements to Assure Safe Use (ETASU)
   - Implementation System
   - Timetable for assessment

3. ETASU EXAMPLES:
   - Healthcare provider training/certification
   - Pharmacy certification
   - Patient enrollment/registry
   - Dispensing limitations
   - Monitoring requirements

4. SHARED SYSTEM REMS:
   - Multiple products share single REMS
   - Example: Opioid Analgesic REMS
   - Single administrator
   - Cost sharing arrangements

5. ASSESSMENT:
   - 18 months, 3 years, 7 years minimum
   - More frequent if specified
   - Evaluate REMS effectiveness
   - Propose modifications if needed

6. INSPECTION FOCUS:
   - REMS implementation completeness
   - ETASU compliance
   - Assessment submission timeliness
   - Effectiveness documentation
regulations.notes: United States - REMS determination factors and shared REMS requirements

regulations.officialIdentifier: FDA Guidance: Postmarketing Adverse Event Reporting for Medical Products
regulations.title: Postmarketing Adverse Event Reporting for Nonprescription Human Drug Products Marketed Without an Approved Application
regulations.summaryDescription: FDA guidance on adverse event reporting for OTC drugs marketed under monograph (without approved NDA). Clarifies that 21 CFR 310.305 applies to these products. Important for companies with OTC portfolios to understand distinct requirements from Rx products. May be subject to inspection even without approved application.
keyRequirements:
1. APPLICABILITY:
   - OTC drugs marketed without approved application
   - OTC monograph products
   - DSCSA applies
   - Different from NDA/ANDA requirements

2. REPORTING REQUIREMENTS - 21 CFR 310.305:
   - 15 days: Serious and unexpected
   - Annual report: All other ADEs
   - Reports go to FAERS

3. RESPONSIBLE PARTIES:
   - Manufacturer
   - Packer
   - Distributor whose name appears on label

4. RECORDKEEPING:
   - Maintain records of all AE reports
   - Retain for 3 years
   - Available for FDA inspection

5. INSPECTION CONSIDERATIONS:
   - FDA can inspect OTC manufacturers
   - AE reporting compliance assessed
   - Written procedures required
   - Training documentation

6. CONSUMER REPORTING:
   - Must provide method for consumers to report
   - Toll-free number or address
   - Process incoming reports
regulations.notes: United States - OTC monograph products without NDA; Different requirements than NDA products

regulations.title: Providing Regulatory Submissions in Electronic Format - FAERS Data Quality Standards
regulations.summaryDescription: FDA expectations for data quality in ICSR submissions to FAERS. Describes common data quality issues FDA identifies and expectations for complete, accurate reports. Inspection findings may cite recurring data quality issues. Understanding FAERS quality metrics helps avoid compliance problems.
keyRequirements:
1. DATA QUALITY ELEMENTS:
   - Completeness of required fields
   - Accuracy of coding (MedDRA)
   - Consistency across reports
   - Timeliness of submission
   - Appropriate seriousness criteria

2. COMMON DEFICIENCIES:
   - Missing/incomplete patient demographics
   - Inadequate event descriptions
   - Incorrect MedDRA term selection
   - Missing onset/resolution dates
   - Incomplete concomitant medications
   - Reporter information gaps

3. NARRATIVE QUALITY:
   - Clear, chronological event description
   - Relevant medical history
   - Treatment and outcome documented
   - Reporter's causality assessment
   - Follow-up attempts documented

4. DUPLICATE DETECTION:
   - Check for duplicates before submission
   - Worldwide unique case ID use
   - Follow-up vs new case determination

5. QUALITY METRICS:
   - FDA tracks submission quality
   - Patterns of poor quality flagged
   - May result in inspection focus

6. REJECTION HANDLING:
   - Monitor acknowledgments
   - Correct and resubmit rejections promptly
   - Track correction patterns
regulations.notes: United States - ICSR data quality requirements for FAERS submissions

regulations.title: GVP Module XVI Addendum I - Risk Minimisation Measures for Medicinal Products with Embryo-Fetal Risks
regulations.summaryDescription:
1. PPP CRITERIA:
   - Proven/strongly suspected teratogenicity
   - Magnitude justifies programme
   - Risk-proportionate approach

2. FEMALE PATIENT REQUIREMENTS:
   - Informed of teratogenic risk
   - Pregnancy testing before/during/after treatment
   - Effective contraception (two methods if highly teratogenic)
   - Written acknowledgment

3. MALE PATIENT REQUIREMENTS:
   - May be required if teratogenic effect via semen
   - Counseling requirements
   - Contraception guidance

4. ADDITIONAL RMM:
   - SmPC/PL updates
   - HCP counseling
   - Educational materials
   - Patient cards

5. TESTING PROTOCOLS:
   - Before treatment initiation
   - During treatment as appropriate
   - After treatment discontinuation

6. EFFECTIVE DATE:
   - 29 August 2025

2. IMPLEMENTATION GUIDANCE:
   - Novel technology adoption
   - Process integration
   - Change management

3. INTERACTIVE TOOLS:
   - Implementation assistance
   - Validation planning
   - Risk assessment

4. INDUSTRY STANDARDS:
   - Reduces duplicative efforts
   - Common approaches
   - Best practices

5. USE CASES:
   - Case intake automation
   - MedDRA coding
   - Duplicate detection
   - Signal detection
regulations.notes: TransCelerate BioPharma initiative providing validation frameworks for AI-based systems in pharmacovigilance. Published guidance on implementing novel technologies in PV. Created interactive tools to assist with implementation. Establishes industry standards and reduces duplicative validation efforts. Helps accelerate responsible AI implementation while addressing economic and validation challenges.

regulations.title: Innovative Designs for Clinical Trials of Cellular and Gene Therapy Products in Small Populations
regulations.summaryDescription:
1. SMALL POPULATION CHALLENGES:
   - Limited patient numbers
   - Safety assessment difficulties
   - Efficacy endpoint selection

2. INNOVATIVE DESIGNS:
   - Adaptive trial designs
   - Bayesian methods
   - Master protocols
   - Platform trials

3. REAL-WORLD EVIDENCE:
   - Natural history data
   - External controls
   - Registry integration

4. SAFETY CONSIDERATIONS:
   - Enhanced monitoring
   - Long-term follow-up
   - Signal detection in small populations

5. RARE DISEASE FOCUS:
   - 80% of rare disease caused by single-gene defects
   - Orphan drug considerations
   - Accelerated pathways

regulations.notes: FDA draft guidance addressing innovative clinical trial designs for cell and gene therapy products in small patient populations. Particularly relevant for rare disease CGT development. Addresses challenges of limited patient numbers in safety assessment. Covers adaptive designs, Bayesian methods, and real-world evidence integration for CGT trials.

regulations.title: EU Artificial Intelligence Act
regulations.summaryDescription:
1. HIGH-RISK AI SYSTEMS:
   - Healthcare applications included
   - Medical device AI
   - Drug safety AI potentially covered

2. REQUIREMENTS:
   - Risk management system
   - Data governance
   - Documentation
   - Human oversight
   - Accuracy/robustness

3. CONFORMITY ASSESSMENT:
   - Self-assessment for some
   - Third-party for others
   - CE marking

4. PV IMPLICATIONS:
   - AI case processing
   - Signal detection tools
   - Automation systems

5. IMPLEMENTATION:
   - Entered force August 2024
   - Phased application 2025-2027
   - Prohibitions: February 2025
   - High-risk: August 2026

6. PENALTIES:
   - Up to €35 million or 7% global turnover
   - Proportionate to violation
keyRequirements:

   - Phased application 2025-2027
   - Prohibitions: February 2025
   - High-risk: August 2026

6. PENALTIES:
   - Up to €35 million or 7% global turnover
   - Proportionate to violation
regulations.notes: EU's comprehensive artificial intelligence regulation. First broad AI law globally. Imposes requirements on high-risk AI systems including those used in healthcare. Relevant for AI applications in pharmacovigilance including case processing automation, signal detection, and benefit-risk assessment. Requires risk assessment, documentation, human oversight, and conformity assessment for high-risk AI.

regulations.title: Post-Marketing All-Case Surveillance (PACS) for Cell and Gene Therapies
regulations.summaryDescription:
1. MANDATORY REQUIREMENT:
   - All approved CGTs
   - All treated patients
   - Comprehensive data collection

2. DATA COLLECTION:
   - Safety data
   - Efficacy data
   - Long-term outcomes

3. REGISTRY:
   - Mandatory enrollment
   - Tracking requirements
   - Follow-up periods

4. RE-EXAMINATION:
   - Defined periods
   - Outcome monitoring
   - Benefit-risk assessment

5. REIMBURSEMENT:
   - Linked to surveillance compliance
   - Eligibility requirements
keyRequirements:
1. MANDATORY REQUIREMENT:
   - All approved CGTs
   - All treated patients
   - Comprehensive data collection

2. DATA COLLECTION:
   - Safety data
   - Efficacy data
   - Long-term outcomes

3. REGISTRY:
   - Mandatory enrollment
   - Tracking requirements
   - Follow-up periods

4. RE-EXAMINATION:
   - Defined periods
   - Outcome monitoring
   - Benefit-risk assessment

5. REIMBURSEMENT:
   - Linked to surveillance compliance
   - Eligibility requirements
regulations.notes: Japan's mandatory post-marketing all-case surveillance (PACS) requirement for approved cell and gene therapies. Requires collection of comprehensive safety and efficacy data from all treated patients. Includes mandatory registry enrollment and re-examination periods for long-term outcome monitoring. Unique to Japan regulatory framework. Critical for sponsors with Japan CGT approvals.

regulations.title: Canada Food and Drugs Act - Pharmacovigilance Requirements
regulations.summaryDescription:
1. SERIOUS ADR REPORTING:
   - 15 calendar days for serious ADRs
   - 30 days for non-serious
   - Canada Vigilance online submission

2. ANNUAL SUMMARY REPORTS:
   - PSUR/PBRER accepted
   - Canadian-specific summary may be requested

3. FOREIGN REPORTING:
   - Serious unexpected foreign ADRs within 15 days

4. RMP:
   - Risk Management Plan per HC guidance
   - Similar to EU RMP structure

5. CONTACT:
   - Must have Canadian representative
   - MedEffect for safety communications
keyRequirements:
1. SERIOUS ADR REPORTING:
   - 15 calendar days for serious ADRs
   - 30 days for non-serious
   - Canada Vigilance online submission

2. ANNUAL SUMMARY REPORTS:
   - PSUR/PBRER accepted
   - Canadian-specific summary may be requested

3. FOREIGN REPORTING:
   - Serious unexpected foreign ADRs within 15 days

4. RMP:
   - Risk Management Plan per HC guidance
   - Similar to EU RMP structure

5. CONTACT:
   - Must have Canadian representative
   - MedEffect for safety communications
regulations.notes: Canadian PV framework under Food and Drugs Act. Requires reporting of serious ADRs within 15 days, all other ADRs within 30 days. Canada Vigilance Program is national ADR database. MedEffect is public safety communication portal. ICH member with aligned requirements.

regulations.title: Guidance Document: Post-Market Surveillance of Natural Health Products and Non-Prescription Drugs
regulations.summaryDescription:
1. REPORTING TIMELINES:
   - Aligned with prescription drug timelines
   - 15 days serious; 30 days non-serious

2. PRODUCT SCOPE:
   - Natural Health Products (NHP)
   - Non-prescription drugs
   - Distinct from Schedule F (Rx)

3. CANADA VIGILANCE:
   - Same reporting portal
   - Product-specific coding
keyRequirements:
1. REPORTING TIMELINES:
   - Aligned with prescription drug timelines
   - 15 days serious

30 days non-serious

2. PRODUCT SCOPE:
   - Natural Health Products (NHP)
   - Non-prescription drugs
   - Distinct from Schedule F (Rx)

3. CANADA VIGILANCE:
   - Same reporting portal
   - Product-specific coding
regulations.notes: Health Canada guidance on post-market surveillance for natural health products and non-prescription drugs. Outlines reporting timelines, case assessment, and signal management specific to these product categories.

regulations.title: Australia Therapeutic Goods Act - Adverse Event Reporting Requirements
regulations.summaryDescription:
1. EXPEDITED REPORTING:
   - 15 calendar days for serious unexpected ADRs
   - 15 days for safety-related labeling changes

2. PERIODIC REPORTING:
   - PSUR/PBRER accepted
   - Australian Specific Annex may be required

3. DAEN:
   - Database of Adverse Event Notifications
   - Publicly searchable

4. SPONSOR OBLIGATIONS:
   - Australian sponsor required
   - Written agreements for PV activities

5. RMP:
   - EU-style RMP accepted
   - Australian-specific requirements possible
keyRequirements:
1. EXPEDITED REPORTING:
   - 15 calendar days for serious unexpected ADRs
   - 15 days for safety-related labeling changes

2. PERIODIC REPORTING:
   - PSUR/PBRER accepted
   - Australian Specific Annex may be required

3. DAEN:
   - Database of Adverse Event Notifications
   - Publicly searchable

4. SPONSOR OBLIGATIONS:
   - Australian sponsor required
   - Written agreements for PV activities

5. RMP:
   - EU-style RMP accepted
   - Australian-specific requirements possible
regulations.notes: Australian PV framework under Therapeutic Goods Act 1989. TGA (Therapeutic Goods Administration) regulates. Sponsors must report serious ADRs within 15 days. PSUR/PBRER requirements. Database of Adverse Event Notifications (DAEN) is public. Strong alignment with ICH guidelines.

regulations.title: Australian Requirements and Recommendations for Pharmacovigilance Responsibilities of Sponsors
regulations.summaryDescription:
1. SPONSOR RESPONSIBILITIES:
   - Maintain PV system
   - Ensure reporting compliance
   - Signal detection and management
   - RMP maintenance

2. REPORTING SYSTEM:
   - TGA Business Services (TBS) portal
   - E2B(R3) format accepted

3. SIGNAL MANAGEMENT:
   - Proactive signal detection
   - Regulatory reporting obligations

4. INSPECTIONS:
   - TGA may conduct PV inspections
   - Similar to EMA/FDA approach
keyRequirements:
1. SPONSOR RESPONSIBILITIES:
   - Maintain PV system
   - Ensure reporting compliance
   - Signal detection and management
   - RMP maintenance

2. REPORTING SYSTEM:

   - E2B(R3) format accepted

3. SIGNAL MANAGEMENT:
   - Proactive signal detection
   - Regulatory reporting obligations

4. INSPECTIONS:
   - TGA may conduct PV inspections
   - Similar to EMA/FDA approach
regulations.notes: Comprehensive TGA guidance on sponsor pharmacovigilance obligations. Covers ICSR reporting, periodic safety reports, signal management, and risk management plans. Aligned with ICH guidelines with Australia-specific requirements.

regulations.title: Swiss Federal Act on Medicinal Products and Medical Devices (Heilmittelgesetz)
regulations.summaryDescription:
1. EXPEDITED REPORTING:
   - 15 days for serious unexpected ADRs
   - Electronic submission via ElViS

2. ICH ADOPTION:
   - ICH guidelines apply automatically at Step 4
   - No separate implementation needed

3. PERIODIC REPORTS:
   - PSUR/PBRER required
   - Aligned with ICH E2C(R2)

4. QPPV:
   - Contact person in Switzerland required
   - Similar to EU QPPV concept

5. EU ALIGNMENT:
   - MRA with EU
   - Accepts EU GVP with modifications
keyRequirements:
1. EXPEDITED REPORTING:
   - 15 days for serious unexpected ADRs
   - Electronic submission via ElViS

2. ICH ADOPTION:
   - ICH guidelines apply automatically at Step 4
   - No separate implementation needed

3. PERIODIC REPORTS:
   - PSUR/PBRER required
   - Aligned with ICH E2C(R2)

4. QPPV:
   - Contact person in Switzerland required
   - Similar to EU QPPV concept

5. EU ALIGNMENT:
   - MRA with EU
   - Accepts EU GVP with modifications
regulations.notes: Swiss PV framework under Therapeutic Products Act (Heilmittelgesetz). Swissmedic regulates. ICH guidelines automatically applicable at Step 4 adoption. Strong EU alignment but separate from EMA. ElViS is Swiss vigilance system.

regulations.title: Swissmedic Guidance Document: Pharmacovigilance Requirements
regulations.summaryDescription:
1. AUTHORIZATION HOLDER OBLIGATIONS:
   - Maintain PV system
   - Qualified person for PV
   - Signal management
   - Reporting compliance

2. ELVIS SYSTEM:
   - Electronic Vigilance System
   - E2B(R3) compatible
   - Mandatory electronic submission

3. RMP:
   - Risk Management Plan required
   - EU format accepted
   - Swiss-specific adaptations possible

4. INSPECTIONS:
   - Swissmedic conducts PV inspections
   - Aligned with PIC/S standards
keyRequirements:

   - Signal management

2. ELVIS SYSTEM:

   - Aligned with PIC/S standards
regulations.notes: Swissmedic guidance document detailing pharmacovigilance requirements for authorization holders. Covers ICSR reporting, periodic reports, signal management, RMP, and inspections. Largely aligned with EU GVP framework.

regulations.title: Protocol Amendments - Safety-Related Changes
regulations.summaryDescription: Establishes requirements for protocol amendments to INDs. Safety-related amendments may be implemented immediately without prior FDA approval when necessary to protect subjects from immediate hazard. Requires submission of amendment describing change and reasons. Critical for sponsors needing to respond rapidly to emerging safety signals during trials.
keyRequirements:
1. AMENDMENT TYPES:
   - New protocol
   - Changes to existing protocol
   - New investigator

2. SAFETY AMENDMENTS:
   - May implement immediately if needed to eliminate apparent immediate hazard
   - Submit amendment within 5 working days
   - Document safety rationale

3. SUBMISSION REQUIREMENTS:
   - Brief description of change
   - Reference to submission containing protocol
   - Reasons for change

4. NON-SAFETY AMENDMENTS:
   - Submit before implementation
   - 30-day review period applies

5. DOCUMENTATION:
   - Maintain records of all amendments
   - IRB notification requirements
regulations.notes: United States - All IND sponsors. Governs protocol amendments including safety-driven changes during clinical development.

regulations.title: Withdrawal of IND
regulations.summaryDescription: Establishes procedures for sponsor withdrawal of an IND. Sponsor may withdraw IND at any time and must notify FDA if clinical investigation discontinued. Critical when safety issues necessitate program termination. Requires notification of all investigators and IRBs when withdrawing for safety reasons.
keyRequirements:
1. WITHDRAWAL NOTIFICATION:
   - Written notification to FDA
   - Reason for withdrawal
   - Date of discontinuation

2. SAFETY-RELATED WITHDRAWAL:
   - Document safety concerns
   - Notify all investigators
   - Notify all IRBs/IECs
   - Ensure subject safety follow-up

3. RECORD RETENTION:
   - Maintain records per 312.62
   - 2 years after IND withdrawn
   - Available for FDA inspection

4. SUBJECT PROTECTION:
   - Ensure appropriate medical care
   - Transition arrangements
   - Safety follow-up as needed
regulations.notes: United States - All IND sponsors. Procedures for voluntary IND withdrawal including safety-related withdrawals.

regulations.title: Expanded Access - Individual Patients
regulations.summaryDescription: Establishes requirements for individual patient expanded access (compassionate use) to investigational drugs. Applies when no comparable alternative therapy available. Safety reporting requirements apply - SAEs must be reported. Critical pathway for providing access while maintaining safety surveillance.
keyRequirements:
1. ELIGIBILITY CRITERIA:
   - Serious or immediately life-threatening condition
   - No comparable alternative therapy
   - Potential benefit justifies potential risks
   - Not interfere with clinical development

2. SAFETY REPORTING:
   - All SAEs reported per 312.32
   - 15-day expedited for serious unexpected
   - 7-day for fatal/life-threatening
   - Annual reports if ongoing

3. PHYSICIAN REQUIREMENTS:
   - Licensed physician
   - IRB review (may be waived for emergency)
   - Informed consent

4. DOCUMENTATION:
   - Treatment protocol
   - Patient records
   - AE documentation
regulations.notes: United States - Sponsors providing expanded access. Compassionate use for individual patients with serious/life-threatening conditions.

regulations.title: Expanded Access - Intermediate-Size Populations
regulations.summaryDescription: Establishes requirements for intermediate-size patient population expanded access. Used when individual patient access inappropriate due to number of patients. Full safety reporting requirements apply. Critical bridge between individual compassionate use and widespread access.
keyRequirements:
1. CRITERIA:
   - Drug being investigated in clinical trial
   - Sufficient safety/efficacy evidence
   - Patients unable to obtain drug in trial
   - No alternative therapy

2. SAFETY REQUIREMENTS:
   - Full IND safety reporting (312.32)
   - Written safety monitoring plan
   - May require DSMB
   - Periodic safety reports

3. PROTOCOL:
   - Treatment protocol required
   - IRB approval
   - Informed consent

4. ANNUAL REPORTING:
   - Safety summary
   - Number of patients treated
   - Outcomes data
regulations.notes: United States - Sponsors providing expanded access to larger patient populations before approval.

regulations.title: Expanded Access - Widespread Treatment Use
regulations.summaryDescription: Establishes requirements for widespread treatment use of investigational drugs. Applies when drug is being investigated or application submitted for serious/life-threatening condition. Enhanced safety reporting and monitoring requirements. Largest scale of pre-approval access.
keyRequirements:
1. CRITERIA:
   - Drug intended for serious/life-threatening condition
   - No satisfactory alternative
   - Drug under investigation in controlled trials OR NDA/BLA submitted
   - Sponsor actively pursuing approval

2. SAFETY REQUIREMENTS:
   - Full IND safety reporting
   - Written procedures for safety monitoring
   - DSMB may be required
   - Periodic safety assessments

3. REPORTING:
   - Annual reports required
   - Safety update reports
   - May be required more frequently

4. CHARGING:
   - May charge for drug under certain conditions
   - Cost recovery provisions
regulations.notes: United States - Sponsors providing widespread treatment access. Treatment IND/protocol for serious conditions.

regulations.title: Foreign Clinical Studies Not Conducted Under an IND
regulations.summaryDescription: Establishes criteria for FDA acceptance of foreign clinical studies not conducted under an IND. Studies must conform to GCP including IEC review. Critical for global drug development - foreign safety data must meet quality standards. FDA may validate data through onsite inspection.
keyRequirements:
1. GCP REQUIREMENTS:
   - Conducted per GCP (ICH E6 or equivalent)
   - IEC/IRB review and approval
   - Informed consent obtained
   - Investigator qualifications

2. DATA QUALITY:
   - Accurate and verifiable from source
   - Site inspection may be required
   - FDA may refuse if data integrity concerns

3. SAFETY DATA:
   - AE/SAE reporting standards
   - Causality assessment
   - Follow-up documentation
   - Consistent with US requirements

4. SUPPORTING DOCUMENTATION:
   - Protocol and amendments
   - IEC/IRB documentation
   - Case report forms
   - Statistical analysis
regulations.notes: United States - Sponsors using foreign study data. Requirements for accepting foreign clinical safety data.

regulations.title: Field Alert Reports
regulations.summaryDescription: Requires NDA/ANDA holders to submit field alert reports within 3 working days of information concerning significant product quality problems. Critical manufacturing/PV interface - product defects may cause adverse events. Includes bacteriological contamination, significant chemical/physical/other change, failure of distributed batches to meet specifications, and any product mix-up.
keyRequirements:
1. REPORTABLE EVENTS:
   - Bacteriological contamination
   - Significant chemical, physical, or other change or deterioration
   - Failure of distributed batch to meet specifications
   - Any mix-up of a distributed product

2. TIMELINE:
   - Within 3 WORKING DAYS of information receipt
   - Urgent - faster than standard AE reporting

3. SUBMISSION:
   - To appropriate FDA district office
   - Include lot numbers, distribution data
   - Investigation findings

4. PV INTERFACE:
   - Quality defects may cause AEs
   - Coordinate field alert with AE monitoring
   - May trigger product recall
   - Cross-reference related AE reports
regulations.notes: United States - All NDA/ANDA holders. 3-day reporting requirement for product quality issues affecting safety.

regulations.title: Withdrawal Procedures - Accelerated Approval
regulations.summaryDescription: Establishes expedited procedures for withdrawal of accelerated approval when post-approval study fails to verify clinical benefit OR when evidence demonstrates product not safe/effective under conditions of use. Critical safety mechanism for drugs approved on surrogate endpoints.
keyRequirements:
1. WITHDRAWAL GROUNDS:
   - Post-approval study fails to verify clinical benefit
   - Evidence demonstrates NOT safe or effective
   - Sponsor fails to conduct required study with due diligence
   - Promotional materials misleading

2. EXPEDITED PROCEDURES:
   - Single hearing opportunity
   - Shorter timelines than standard withdrawal
   - FDA may withdraw before hearing in some cases

3. SAFETY WITHDRAWAL:
   - New safety information emerges
   - B-R no longer favorable
   - May be initiated by FDA or sponsor

4. SPONSOR OPTIONS:
   - Voluntary withdrawal
   - Request hearing
   - Propose REMS or other risk mitigation
regulations.notes: United States - Products approved under accelerated approval (Subpart H). FDA withdrawal procedures for safety concerns.

regulations.title: Accelerated Approval of Biological Products
regulations.summaryDescription: Parallel to 21 CFR 314 Subpart H for biological products. Allows approval based on surrogate or intermediate clinical endpoint with required post-approval confirmatory studies. Enhanced PV obligations apply. FDA may withdraw if clinical benefit not verified or safety concerns arise.
keyRequirements:
1. APPROVAL BASIS:
   - Surrogate endpoint reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit
   - Intermediate clinical endpoint
   - Serious or life-threatening condition
   - Meaningful advantage over existing treatments

2. POST-APPROVAL REQUIREMENTS:
   - Confirmatory study to verify clinical benefit
   - Due diligence in study conduct
   - Progress reports to FDA
   - Enhanced safety monitoring

3. WITHDRAWAL PROCEDURES:
   - Per 601.43 if study fails to verify benefit
   - If sponsor fails to conduct study with due diligence
   - If evidence shows not safe/effective

4. PV IMPLICATIONS:
   - Enhanced signal detection
   - More frequent safety reporting
   - Risk management considerations
regulations.notes: United States - Biological products approved under accelerated approval. Post-market safety study requirements.

regulations.title: Good Laboratory Practice for Nonclinical Laboratory Studies
regulations.summaryDescription: Establishes requirements for conducting nonclinical laboratory studies that support applications for research or marketing permits. Foundation for safety assessment - toxicology, carcinogenicity, reproductive toxicity studies must comply. Data integrity and study conduct requirements ensure reliable safety predictions.
keyRequirements:
1. APPLICABILITY:
   - Safety studies for IND, NDA, BLA, ANDA
   - Toxicology studies
   - Carcinogenicity studies
   - Reproductive toxicity
   - Genotoxicity studies

2. KEY REQUIREMENTS:
   - Study director responsibilities
   - Quality assurance unit
   - Written protocols
   - Standard operating procedures
   - Test article characterization

3. DATA INTEGRITY:
   - Raw data recording
   - Data storage and retrieval
   - Archive requirements
   - Study reconstruction capability

4. REPORTING:
   - Final study report content
   - Amendments and corrections
   - Sponsor notification of problems

5. PV RELEVANCE:
   - Predicts clinical safety signals
   - IB safety information source
   - Supports benefit-risk assessment
regulations.notes: United States - All nonclinical safety studies intended to support FDA submissions. Foundation for preclinical safety assessment.

regulations.title: Medication Guides
regulations.summaryDescription: Establishes requirements for Medication Guides - FDA-approved patient labeling distributed with certain prescription drugs. Required when FDA determines information necessary to prevent serious adverse effects, when patient adherence significantly affects efficacy, or when product has serious risk requiring patient decision-making. Critical risk communication tool.
keyRequirements:
1. WHEN REQUIRED:
   - Drug poses serious, significant public health concern requiring patient information
   - Patient labeling could help prevent serious AEs
   - Product has serious risk(s) patient should weigh against benefits
   - Patient adherence to directions is crucial to drug's effectiveness

2. CONTENT REQUIREMENTS:
   - Standardized format
   - Uses approved language
   - Specific safety information
   - Written in patient-friendly language
   - Readable by patients with limited literacy

3. DISTRIBUTION:
   - Dispensed with each prescription
   - Pharmacist responsibility
   - Outpatient settings
   - Inpatient at discharge

4. REMS RELATIONSHIP:
   - May be element of REMS
   - Can be required outside REMS
   - FDA reviews and approves content
regulations.notes: United States - Products requiring Medication Guides. Patient-directed labeling for serious safety concerns.

regulations.title: Prescription Drug Advertising - Labeling Requirements
regulations.summaryDescription: Establishes that prescription drugs are exempt from certain labeling requirements ONLY IF conditions are met, including that labeling bears adequate information for safe use. Requires all advertising to contain true statement of safety information. Critical intersection of labeling, advertising, and safety communication.
keyRequirements:
1. EXEMPTION CONDITIONS:
   - Dispensed on valid prescription
   - Labeling bears adequate directions for use
   - Labeling contains safety information

2. SAFETY INFORMATION REQUIREMENTS:
   - Adequate warnings
   - Contraindications
   - Side effects
   - Precautions

3. ADVERTISING REQUIREMENTS:
   - True statement of information in brief summary
   - Fair balance of efficacy and risk
   - No false or misleading claims

4. PV INTERFACE:
   - Labeling must be updated with new safety information
   - Advertising must reflect current labeling
   - Safety claims must be supported by data
regulations.notes: United States - All prescription drug products. Requirements for prescription drug labeling and advertising.

regulations.title: Prescription Drug Advertising
regulations.summaryDescription: Comprehensive regulation of prescription drug advertising. Requires advertisements to present fair balance of efficacy and risk information. Prohibits false or misleading claims. Requires brief summary of safety information. Critical for ensuring safety information reaches prescribers and patients through promotional channels.
keyRequirements:
1. ADVERTISEMENT TYPES:
   - Reminder advertisements (limited)
   - Product claim advertisements (full)
   - Help-seeking advertisements

2. FAIR BALANCE:
   - Risk information equal prominence
   - Cannot minimize risks
   - Contraindications, warnings, precautions, adverse reactions

3. BRIEF SUMMARY:
   - Includes side effects, contraindications, effectiveness
   - Must be truthful and not misleading
   - Reference to full prescribing information

4. PROHIBITED CONTENT:
   - False or misleading representations
   - Failure to reveal material facts
   - Lack of fair balance

5. DISSEMINATION:
   - Professional publications
   - Direct-to-consumer (DTC)
   - Internet advertising
   - Social media
regulations.notes: United States - All prescription drug products. Detailed requirements for prescription drug advertising content.

regulations.title: Written Procedures; Deviations
regulations.summaryDescription: Requires written procedures for production and process control designed to assure drug products meet specifications. Deviations from written procedures must be documented, justified, and approved. Critical manufacturing/PV interface - deviations may affect product safety and require AE monitoring.
keyRequirements:
1. WRITTEN PROCEDURES:
   - Production and process control
   - Designed to assure specifications met
   - Followed during manufacturing
   - Documented execution

2. DEVIATION REQUIREMENTS:
   - Any deviation recorded and justified
   - Approved by quality control unit
   - Impact assessment required
   - May require investigation per 211.192

3. PV INTERFACE:
   - Deviations may cause product quality issues
   - Product quality issues may cause AEs
   - Cross-functional review for safety impact
   - May trigger field alert or recall

4. DOCUMENTATION:
   - Batch records
   - Deviation forms
   - Investigation reports
   - CAPA documentation
regulations.notes: United States - Drug manufacturers. Production and process controls including deviation handling.

regulations.title: Sampling and Testing of In-Process Materials and Drug Products
regulations.summaryDescription: Requires written procedures for in-process sampling and testing to monitor and validate drug product performance. Ensures batch uniformity and integrity. Critical for detecting manufacturing problems before distribution - in-process failures may indicate safety concerns.
keyRequirements:
1. WRITTEN PROCEDURES:
   - In-process specifications
   - Sampling procedures
   - Testing methods
   - Acceptance criteria

2. VALIDATION:
   - In-process procedures validated
   - Designed to assure uniformity
   - Detect problems during production

3. TESTING REQUIREMENTS:
   - Tablet/capsule weight variation
   - Disintegration time
   - Adequacy of mixing
   - Dissolution rates
   - Content uniformity

4. PV RELEVANCE:
   - In-process failures may indicate safety issues
   - Batch rejection protects patients
   - OOS results require investigation
   - Trend analysis may identify emerging problems
regulations.notes: United States - Drug manufacturers. In-process testing requirements for product quality and safety.

regulations.title: Expiration Dating
regulations.summaryDescription: Requires expiration date based on stability testing. Ensures drugs meet specifications throughout shelf life. Critical safety provision - degradation products may be toxic or drug may lose efficacy. PV may see reports of problems with products near expiration.
keyRequirements:
1. REQUIREMENTS:
   - Expiration date on all drug products
   - Based on stability testing
   - Not exceed date supported by stability data

2. STABILITY TESTING:
   - Appropriate sample size
   - Adequate test intervals
   - Reliable, meaningful, specific test methods
   - Storage conditions representative of product storage

3. HOMEOPATHIC EXEMPTION:
   - Exempt from expiration dating
   - If otherwise exempt from CGMPs

4. PV RELEVANCE:
   - Expired products may degrade
   - Degradation products may cause AEs
   - Loss of efficacy may lead to therapeutic failure
   - Reports of problems near expiration warrant investigation
regulations.notes: United States - Drug manufacturers. Expiration dating requirements based on stability.

regulations.title: Drug-Induced Liver Injury: Premarketing Clinical Evaluation
regulations.summaryDescription: Guidance on monitoring for and evaluating drug-induced liver injury during clinical development. Introduces Hy's Law as predictor of severe DILI. Defines criteria for ALT elevations requiring attention. Critical for identifying hepatotoxicity signals during development and post-market.
keyRequirements:
1. HY'S LAW CRITERIA:
   - ALT or AST >3x ULN
   - Total bilirubin >2x ULN
   - No other cause found
   - Predictive of 10-50% mortality

2. MONITORING RECOMMENDATIONS:
   - Baseline liver tests
   - Periodic monitoring schedule
   - Stopping rules for elevations
   - Rechallenge considerations

3. SIGNAL EVALUATION:
   - Establish baseline rates
   - Compare to control/comparator
   - Causality assessment
   - Dose-response relationship

4. LABELING IMPLICATIONS:
   - Warnings for hepatotoxicity
   - Monitoring recommendations
   - Contraindications if severe risk

5. POST-MARKET MONITORING:
   - Signal detection for DILI
   - Hy's Law cases require expedited reporting
   - Registry considerations
regulations.notes: United States - All drug/biologic development programs. Guidance on detecting and evaluating hepatotoxicity signals (DILI).

regulations.title: Clinical Evaluation of QT/QTc Interval Prolongation and Proarrhythmic Potential
regulations.summaryDescription: Guidance on evaluating potential for QT/QTc interval prolongation and proarrhythmic effects. Describes thorough QT study design (TQT). Defines threshold of regulatory concern. Critical for cardiac safety assessment - QT prolongation can cause Torsades de Pointes and sudden death.
keyRequirements:
1. THRESHOLD OF CONCERN:
   - Mean QTc prolongation >10 ms
   - Triggers need for further evaluation
   - Upper bound of 95% CI

2. TQT STUDY DESIGN:
   - Randomized, double-blind
   - Positive control (moxifloxacin)
   - Therapeutic and supratherapeutic doses
   - ECG-blood concentration relationship

3. ALTERNATIVE APPROACHES (E14 Q&As):
   - Concentration-QTc analysis
   - May replace TQT study in some cases
   - Requires robust Phase 1 ECG data

4. LABELING:
   - QT prolongation warning if positive
   - Contraindications for high-risk
   - Drug interaction warnings (CYP inhibitors)

5. POST-MARKET:
   - Monitor for arrhythmia reports
   - Torsades de Pointes signal detection
   - Drug interaction surveillance
regulations.notes: United States/ICH - All drug development programs. Cardiac safety assessment for QT prolongation risk.

regulations.title: Suicidality in Drug Evaluation: Assessment and Reporting
regulations.summaryDescription: Guidance on prospectively assessing suicidal ideation and behavior (SIB) in clinical trials. Recommends Columbia-Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS). Critical for CNS drugs and any drugs that may affect mood or behavior. Important signal detection focus post-market.
keyRequirements:
1. WHEN TO ASSESS:
   - CNS drugs (psychiatric, neurological)
   - Drugs affecting appetite, weight
   - Drugs with CNS side effects
   - Drugs for conditions associated with suicidality

2. RECOMMENDED INSTRUMENT:
   - Columbia-Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS)
   - Validated scale
   - Captures ideation and behavior
   - Administered at baseline and throughout trial

3. SIGNAL CATEGORIES:
   - Suicidal ideation (passive, active)
   - Suicidal behavior (attempt, interrupted, aborted)
   - Preparatory acts
   - Completed suicide

4. ANALYSIS:
   - Pre-specified analysis plan
   - Compare to placebo/comparator
   - Time-to-event analysis
   - Subgroup analyses (age, baseline risk)

5. LABELING:
   - Boxed warning if significant risk
   - Class-wide warnings (antidepressants, antiepileptics)
regulations.notes: United States - CNS drugs and others affecting psychiatric conditions. Guidance on assessing suicidality signals.

regulations.title: Medication Error Reports: Postmarketing
regulations.summaryDescription: Guidance on submitting medication error reports to FAERS. Requires reporting of medication errors that result in adverse events. Recommends reporting errors without harm to identify and prevent future harm. Critical for use-related safety signal detection.
keyRequirements:
1. REPORTABLE ERRORS:
   - Errors resulting in adverse events (required)
   - Errors intercepted before reaching patient (recommended)
   - Errors that reached patient but caused no harm (recommended)
   - Near-misses (recommended)

2. ERROR CATEGORIES:
   - Wrong drug
   - Wrong dose/strength
   - Wrong patient
   - Wrong route
   - Wrong time/frequency
   - Monitoring errors

3. ROOT CAUSES TO DOCUMENT:
   - Look-alike/sound-alike names
   - Labeling/packaging confusion
   - Abbreviation misinterpretation
   - Device/delivery system issues

4. REPORTING:
   - Via FAERS/MedWatch
   - Include product details
   - Describe error circumstances
   - Patient outcome

5. SAFETY SIGNAL:
   - Patterns may indicate labeling issues
   - May require labeling changes
   - May require REMS
regulations.notes: United States - All marketed products. Guidance on reporting medication errors including those without patient harm.

regulations.title: Immunogenicity Assessment for Therapeutic Protein Products
regulations.summaryDescription: Guidance on immunogenicity assessment for therapeutic protein products. Addresses anti-drug antibody (ADA) development and clinical consequences. Critical for biologics PV - immunogenic reactions range from injection site reactions to severe hypersensitivity and loss of efficacy.
keyRequirements:
1. IMMUNOGENICITY ASSESSMENT:
   - Risk-based approach
   - Product-related factors (structure, formulation)
   - Patient-related factors (immune status, genetics)
   - Treatment-related factors (route, dose, duration)

2. ADA TESTING:
   - Screening assay (sensitive)
   - Confirmatory assay (specific)
   - Titer determination
   - Neutralizing antibody (NAb) assay

3. CLINICAL CONSEQUENCES:
   - Injection site reactions
   - Infusion reactions
   - Hypersensitivity/anaphylaxis
   - Loss of efficacy
   - Cross-reactivity with endogenous proteins

4. POST-MARKET MONITORING:
   - Signal detection for immune-mediated events
   - ADA prevalence monitoring
   - Product-specific surveillance

5. LABELING:
   - Immunogenicity information
   - Incidence data from trials
   - Comparison limitations
regulations.notes: United States - Therapeutic protein products including biologics and biosimilars. Immunogenicity assessment and monitoring.

regulations.title: Clinical Lactation Studies: Considerations for Study Design
regulations.summaryDescription: Guidance on designing and conducting clinical lactation studies. Supports PLLR (Pregnancy and Lactation Labeling Rule) compliance. Addresses drug transfer into breast milk, infant dose estimation, and risk assessment. Critical for products likely to be used during breastfeeding.
keyRequirements:
1. STUDY DESIGN:
   - Measure drug levels in breast milk
   - Estimate relative infant dose (RID)
   - Assess infant exposure
   - Consider active metabolites

2. SAMPLING CONSIDERATIONS:
   - Timing relative to dose
   - Milk composition changes
   - Steady-state conditions
   - Multiple time points

3. RISK ASSESSMENT:
   - RID typically <10% considered acceptable
   - Consider infant oral bioavailability
   - Infant developmental stage
   - Drug toxicity profile

4. LABELING (PLLR):
   - Section 8.2 Lactation
   - Risk summary
   - Clinical considerations
   - Data section

5. POST-MARKET:
   - Monitor for infant adverse events
   - Lactation registries
   - Signal detection in nursing infants
regulations.notes: United States - Products used by lactating women. Guidance on lactation studies for PLLR compliance.

regulations.title: Real-World Evidence Program
regulations.summaryDescription: Framework and guidance documents on using real-world data (RWD) and real-world evidence (RWE) in regulatory decisions. Includes safety signal detection, PMR/PMC fulfillment, and label expansion. Growing importance for post-market safety surveillance beyond traditional spontaneous reporting.
keyRequirements:
1. RWD SOURCES:
   - Electronic health records (EHR)
   - Claims and billing data
   - Registries
   - Patient-generated data
   - Mobile devices/wearables

2. RWE APPLICATIONS:
   - Safety signal detection and evaluation
   - PMR/PMC study designs
   - Label expansion for new indications
   - REMS effectiveness assessment

3. DATA QUALITY CONSIDERATIONS:
   - Relevance to regulatory question
   - Data reliability and accuracy
   - Completeness
   - Transparency of methods

4. STUDY DESIGNS:
   - Observational cohort studies
   - Self-controlled designs
   - Target trial emulation
   - Nested case-control

5. FDA PROGRAMS:
   - Sentinel System
   - FDA-Catalyst
   - RWE demonstration projects
regulations.notes: United States - All products. Framework for using real-world evidence in regulatory decision-making including safety.

regulations.title: Rare Pediatric Disease Priority Review Vouchers
regulations.summaryDescription: Guidance on Rare Pediatric Disease Priority Review Voucher program. Products approved for rare pediatric diseases may receive voucher. Relevant for PV because these products often have limited safety databases and require enhanced post-market surveillance.
keyRequirements:
1. ELIGIBILITY:
   - Rare pediatric disease (affects <200,000 in US)
   - Primarily affects ages 0-18
   - Serious or life-threatening
   - New molecular entity or biologic

2. VOUCHER BENEFITS:
   - Priority review for subsequent application
   - Transferable to other sponsors
   - Significant commercial value

3. PV IMPLICATIONS:
   - Limited pediatric safety database at approval
   - Enhanced post-market surveillance often required
   - PMR/PMC likely for additional pediatric safety data
   - Signal detection critical in small populations

4. SAFETY MONITORING:
   - Enhanced reporting encouraged
   - Registry participation may be required
   - Long-term developmental safety follow-up
regulations.notes: United States - Rare pediatric disease products. Incentive program with safety monitoring implications.

regulations.title: Postmarketing Studies and Clinical Trials: Implementation of FDAAA
regulations.summaryDescription: Comprehensive guidance on implementation of postmarketing study and clinical trial requirements under FDAAA Section 505(o)(3). Details categories of studies, status reporting, milestone requirements, and FDA enforcement authority. Critical for understanding PMR/PMC obligations.
keyRequirements:
1. PMR vs PMC:
   - PMR: Required by statute (FDAAA 505(o)(3))
   - PMC: Agreed upon commitment at approval
   - Different enforcement implications

2. STATUS REPORTING:
   - Annual status reports within 60 days of approval anniversary
   - Status categories: Pending, Ongoing, Submitted, Completed, Released, Terminated
   - Milestone tracking

3. ENFORCEMENT (PMRs):
   - Warning letter
   - Civil money penalties (up to $250K/day, $1M total)
   - Failure to submit or complete
   - Misbranding action

4. STUDY TYPES:
   - Clinical safety studies
   - Nonclinical safety studies
   - Observational studies
   - Labeling studies

5. TIMETABLE:
   - Proposed schedule at approval
   - Milestones: Protocol submission, enrollment, completion
   - Renegotiation process
regulations.notes: United States - All NDA/BLA/ANDA holders. Detailed guidance on PMR/PMC requirements and status reporting.

regulations.title: Patient-Focused Drug Development: Selecting, Developing, or Modifying Fit-for-Purpose Clinical Outcome Assessments
regulations.summaryDescription: Guidance on developing clinical outcome assessments (COAs) including patient-reported outcomes (PROs). Relevant for PV as patient-reported tolerability and side effect experiences are increasingly important in benefit-risk assessment and post-market safety monitoring.
keyRequirements:
1. COA TYPES:
   - Patient-reported outcome (PRO)
   - Clinician-reported outcome (ClinRO)
   - Observer-reported outcome (ObsRO)
   - Performance outcome (PerfO)

2. SAFETY/TOLERABILITY PROs:
   - Patient experience of side effects
   - Symptom severity rating
   - Impact on daily functioning
   - Treatment satisfaction/burden

3. FIT-FOR-PURPOSE:
   - Context of use defined
   - Measurement properties established
   - Validation for intended use

4. PV APPLICATIONS:
   - Patient-reported AE collection
   - Treatment burden assessment
   - Quality of life impact of AEs
   - Post-market safety surveys

5. LABELING:
   - PRO-LAM for patient labeling claims
   - Safety information from patient perspective
regulations.notes: United States - All drug development programs. Guidance on patient-reported outcomes including safety/tolerability.

regulations.title: Clinical Trial Diversity
regulations.summaryDescription: Guidance on improving diversity in clinical trial enrollment. Relevant for PV because safety profiles may differ across populations. Subgroup analyses by race/ethnicity, age, sex are increasingly required. Post-market surveillance must monitor for population-specific safety signals.
keyRequirements:
1. DIVERSITY REQUIREMENTS:
   - Race and Ethnicity Action Plan
   - Enrollment targets reflecting disease epidemiology
   - Justified exclusion criteria only
   - Decentralized trial elements to improve access

2. SAFETY SUBGROUP ANALYSIS:
   - Pre-specified by demographic groups
   - Race/ethnicity subgroups
   - Age subgroups (pediatric, geriatric)
   - Sex subgroups

3. POST-MARKET IMPLICATIONS:
   - Monitor for population-specific safety signals
   - Health disparities in adverse event patterns
   - Label updates for population-specific risks

4. FDORA REQUIREMENTS:
   - Diversity Action Plan required for Phase 3
   - Submitted with IND
   - Justification for enrollment targets

5. LABELING:
   - Subgroup data in Clinical Studies section
   - Population-specific warnings if warranted
regulations.notes: United States - All clinical development programs. Diversity requirements with safety subgroup analysis implications.

regulations.title: Diversity Action Plans to Improve Enrollment in Clinical Studies
regulations.summaryDescription: Implements FDORA Section 3601 requirement for Diversity Action Plans for Phase 3 and other pivotal trials. Sponsors must submit plan describing how enrollment will reflect disease demographics. Critical for ensuring safety data generalizable to intended patient population.
keyRequirements:
1. FDORA REQUIREMENTS:
   - Diversity Action Plan for Phase 3/pivotal
   - Submitted to IND before Phase 3 initiation
   - Enrollment goals for underrepresented groups
   - Rationale for goals based on disease epidemiology

2. PLAN CONTENTS:
   - Enrollment goals by demographic group
   - Strategies to meet goals
   - Site selection considerations
   - Decentralization elements

3. SAFETY IMPLICATIONS:
   - Adequate safety data across populations
   - Pre-specified subgroup analyses
   - Power for safety assessments
   - Population-specific signals detectable

4. FDA OVERSIGHT:
   - Review of diversity plan
   - May request modifications
   - Monitor enrollment during trial
   - Assess at NDA/BLA submission

5. POST-MARKET:
   - PMR for additional populations if gaps
   - Signal detection by subgroup
   - Label updates as data accumulates
regulations.notes: United States - All Phase 3 clinical trials. FDORA Section 3601 implementing guidance on diversity action plans.

regulations.title: Definitions - Biological Products
regulations.summaryDescription: Provides definitions for biological products regulation. Defines key terms including biological product, lot, potency, safety, purity, and sterility. Foundation for understanding biologics-specific safety requirements. Critical for PV to correctly classify products and apply appropriate requirements.
keyRequirements:
1. KEY DEFINITIONS:
   - Biological product: Virus, therapeutic serum, toxin, antitoxin, vaccine, blood, blood component/derivative, allergenic product, protein, analogous product
   - Safety: Relative freedom from harmful effect
   - Purity: Relative freedom from extraneous matter
   - Potency: Specific ability to produce given result
   - Sterility: Freedom from viable contaminating microorganisms

2. SAFETY DEFINITION:
   - Direct or indirect harmful effect
   - Considered relative not absolute
   - Evaluated in conditions of recommended use

3. PV RELEVANCE:
   - Product classification affects PV requirements
   - Safety definition informs benefit-risk
   - Purity/potency deviations may cause AEs

4. INTERCHANGEABLE BIOLOGICAL PRODUCT:
   - Biosimilar that may be substituted
   - Enhanced PV considerations
regulations.notes: United States - All biological products. Key definitions for biologics regulation including safety-related terms.

regulations.title: Establishment Standards - Biologics
regulations.summaryDescription: Establishes standards for licensed biological product manufacturing establishments. Includes personnel qualifications, physical standards, equipment, and records. Manufacturing quality directly impacts product safety - deviations may cause adverse events.
keyRequirements:
1. PERSONNEL (600.10):
   - Qualified personnel
   - Training requirements
   - Health requirements
   - Supervision standards

2. PHYSICAL ESTABLISHMENT (600.11):
   - Adequate facilities
   - Separate areas to prevent contamination
   - Environmental controls
   - Clean room requirements

3. EQUIPMENT (600.11):
   - Suitable design and construction
   - Adequate maintenance
   - Calibration requirements
   - Cleaning procedures

4. RECORDS (600.12):
   - Batch production records
   - Distribution records (critical for traceability)
   - Retention requirements

5. PV INTERFACE:
   - Manufacturing deviations → product quality → AEs
   - Traceability for batch-specific safety issues
   - Contamination events require PV assessment
regulations.notes: United States - Licensed biologic manufacturing establishments. Facility standards affecting product safety.

regulations.title: Records and Reports - Postmarketing Safety
regulations.summaryDescription: Statutory authority for FDA to require post-approval records and safety reports. Foundation for 21 CFR 314.80 and 314.81 postmarketing reporting regulations. Grants FDA authority to access records and require safety submissions. Critical legal basis for PV obligations.
keyRequirements:
1. STATUTORY AUTHORITY:
   - FDA may require records and reports
   - Includes adverse event reports
   - Includes any other necessary information
   - Published in Federal Register

2. INFORMATION TYPES:
   - Clinical experience
   - Studies
   - Reports
   - Data on safety and efficacy

3. FDA ACCESS:
   - May require access to records
   - Inspection authority
   - Copy authority

4. IMPLEMENTING REGULATIONS:
   - 21 CFR 314.80 (adverse experience reporting)
   - 21 CFR 314.81 (other reports)
   - 21 CFR 314.98 (ANDA requirements)

5. ENFORCEMENT:
   - Failure to report = violation
   - May result in warning letter
   - May affect application status
regulations.notes: United States - All NDA holders. Statutory authority for FDA to require adverse event reporting and records.

regulations.title: Safety Labeling Changes - FDA Authority
regulations.summaryDescription: Grants FDA authority to require safety labeling changes when new safety information becomes available. Includes authority to require labeling changes, conduct REMS assessment, and hold public meetings on safety issues. Critical enforcement mechanism for ensuring labels reflect current safety knowledge.
keyRequirements:
1. FDA AUTHORITY:
   - Require labeling change based on new safety information
   - Issue order for specific changes
   - Initiate labeling change discussions
   - Require REMS modifications

2. NEW SAFETY INFORMATION:
   - Information derived from:
     • Clinical trials
     • Adverse event reports
     • Postmarketing studies
     • Peer-reviewed literature
     • FDA analysis

3. PROCESS:
   - FDA notifies holder of need for change
   - Holder submits supplement
   - If holder disagrees, can request discussion
   - FDA may order change if impasse

4. ENFORCEMENT:
   - Product considered misbranded if label not updated
   - Civil money penalties possible

5. HOLDER OBLIGATIONS:
   - Review new safety information promptly
   - Update labeling without waiting for FDA order
   - CBE-0 supplement for safety changes
regulations.notes: United States - All approved drug products. FDA authority to require safety labeling changes.

regulations.title: FDA Adverse Event Reporting System
regulations.summaryDescription: FDA's post-market safety surveillance database. Contains adverse event reports and medication error reports from manufacturers, healthcare professionals, and consumers. FDA uses FAERS for signal detection, benefit-risk assessment, and regulatory action support. Understanding FAERS is critical for sponsors.
keyRequirements:
1. DATA SOURCES:
   - Mandatory reports from manufacturers (21 CFR 314.80, 600.80)
   - Voluntary reports from HCPs and consumers (MedWatch)
   - Foreign reports
   - Literature reports

2. FDA USES:
   - Signal detection
   - Safety reviews
   - Support for regulatory actions
   - Advisory committee briefings
   - Drug Safety Communications

3. PUBLIC ACCESS:
   - FAERS Public Dashboard
   - Quarterly data files
   - FOIA requests for specific data

4. LIMITATIONS:
   - Underreporting
   - No denominator (usage data)
   - Cannot establish causality alone
   - Duplicate reports possible
   - Quality varies

5. SPONSOR IMPLICATIONS:
   - Monitor FAERS for own products
   - Understand FDA signal detection methods
   - Be prepared to respond to FDA queries
   - Compare company data to FAERS patterns
regulations.notes: United States - All marketed products. FDA's post-market safety surveillance database and signal detection system.

regulations.title: Vaccine Adverse Event Reporting System
regulations.summaryDescription: National vaccine safety surveillance program co-managed by FDA and CDC. Collects reports of adverse events following vaccination. Vaccine manufacturers required to report per 21 CFR 600.80. Critical for vaccine PV - distinct from FAERS with vaccine-specific analysis capabilities.
keyRequirements:
1. REPORTING SOURCES:
   - Manufacturers (required per 600.80)
   - Healthcare providers (required per NCVIA for table events)
   - Patients/caregivers (voluntary)

2. NCVIA TABLE EVENTS:
   - Specific events required to report
   - Within specified timeframes
   - Examples: anaphylaxis, encephalopathy, seizures

3. CDC/FDA ROLES:
   - CDC: System administration, data analysis
   - FDA: Regulatory oversight, signal assessment
   - Joint signal review

4. VAERS DATA:
   - Publicly searchable (VAERS Wonder)
   - Weekly updates
   - Individual case details available
   - MedDRA coded

5. VACCINE MANUFACTURER OBLIGATIONS:
   - Report all AEs learned of (serious and non-serious)
   - 15-day expedited for deaths
   - Follow-up information
   - Annual summary not required (unlike drugs)
regulations.notes: United States - All licensed vaccines. National vaccine safety surveillance system co-managed by FDA/CDC.

regulations.title: Protocol Amendments
regulations.summaryDescription: Establishes requirements for amending IND protocols. Safety-driven protocol amendments require expedited submission when changes are needed to eliminate apparent immediate hazards to subjects. Defines categories of amendments and submission timelines based on safety impact.
